mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the French Revolution (A.1.b) including the Third Estate revolt and Jacobins (A.1.b.i), the First Republic with Danton, Robespierre, and the Terror (A.1.b.ii), and the Directory and rise of Napoleon (A.1.b.iii).

Covers the War of the First Coalition (A.1.c.i), Second Coalition campaigns (A.1.c.ii), Third Coalition battles (A.1.c.iii), the Peninsular War and Russian defeat (A.1.c.iv), Waterloo and Congress of Vienna (A.1.c.v), French occupation effects (A.1.d), Confederation of the Rhine (A.1.i), Spain's war of independence (A.1.j.i), Italy during the Revolution (A.1.l), and connections to the 1848 revolutions (B.2.a).

Napoleon's rise from first consul to emperor (A.1.b.iii), France under the Napoleonic Empire (A.1.b.iv), international wars reshaping European boundaries (A.1.c), and collapse and exile at Waterloo (A.1.c.v).

Covers British commercial growth, factory system, textiles, and railroads (A.2.a.i), spread to Belgium, France, and Germany (A.2.a.ii), social consequences including capitalist-worker division (A.2.a.iii), and industrialization 1870-1914 (B.1.d).

Covers the Serbian crisis and trench warfare (B.2.d.i), entrance of other nations (B.2.d.ii), submarine warfare, U.S. entry, and Russian collapse (B.2.d.iii), total war (B.2.d.iv), strategic plans (B.2.d.v), and the Paris Peace Conference (B.2.d.vi).
Directly covers the Paris Peace Conference, Wilson's influence, German reparations, and the League of Nations (B.2.d.vi).
The 1917 Russian Revolution connects to the Russian exit from WWI (B.2.d.iii) and abolition of the monarchy (B.3.e.iv).

Covers Russia under Alexander I and Napoleonic Wars (A.1.f), later conservatism and the Decembrist revolt (A.2.o), bureaucratic rule (A.2.o.i), foreign policy with Poland and Turkey (A.2.o.ii), Crimean War and abolition of serfdom (B.3.e.i), Alexander III and Nicholas II (B.3.e.ii), economic and cultural developments (B.3.e.iii), and the 1917 abolition of monarchy (B.3.e.iv).

Covers Austria in coalitions against Napoleon (A.1.h), national consciousness among Habsburg peoples (A.2.l.i), Metternich's hostility to liberalism (A.2.l.ii), 1848-49 revolutions (A.2.l.iii), the Ausgleich and Dual Monarchy (B.3.d.i), Austria's liberal ascendancy (B.3.d.ii), Hungary under the Compromise (B.3.d.iii), and 1918 dissolution (B.3.d.iv).

National self-determination connects to cultural nationalism legacy of the French Revolution (A.2.b), national consciousness among Habsburg peoples (A.2.l.i), and Italian and German unification (B.2.a).
Marx's philosophy connects to social consequences of the Industrial Revolution (A.2.a), German Idealism and its disciples (A.2.e), and determinism and materialism (B.1.a).
Socialism's 19th-century origins connect to political and social thought (B.1.a) and Fabian socialism and the Labour movement in Britain (B.3.a.iii).
Communist ideology through the Russian Revolution connects to the 1917 abolition of the monarchy (B.3.e.iv).
Democratic theory connects to patriotism in America and the Netherlands (A.1.a) and British political reform measures (A.2.i).
Comparative revolutionary theory covers the French Revolution (A.1), European revolutions of 1848 (A.2.h.ii), the Frankfurt Assembly's failure (A.2.k.ii), and Italian revolutions of 1848 (A.2.m.iv).
European imperialism's resurgence (B.2.c) including new colonial powers and the scramble for Africa.

mappings.vsiAuthor: Christopher Harvie, H. C. G. Matthew
mappings.rationaleAI: Covers Britain 1789-1815 including English radicalism (A.1.e.i), reform measures including the Reform Bill and Corn Laws (A.2.i, A.2.ii), mid-Victorian politics (B.3.a.i), Victorian society (B.3.a.ii), Gladstone and the Irish Question (B.3.a.iii), Lloyd George's reforms (B.3.a.iv), and WWI participation (B.3.a.v).
Covers the Franco-Prussian War, Dreyfus Affair, and Third Republic (B.3.b.ii), and broader French history (B.3.b).
Covers Risorgimento and 1861 unification (B.3.f.i), the Kingdom of Italy with socialism and labor movements (B.3.f.ii).
The Romantic movement emphasizing individualism and nature in contrast with the Enlightenment (A.2.c).
Liberal political theory connects to British reform measures (A.2.i) and Gladstone's liberalism (B.3.a.iii).
Clausewitz's military theory from the Napoleonic Wars connects to international war (A.1.c) and Prussian leadership in wars of liberation (A.1.g.ii).
Hegel's dialectical philosophy directly addresses the role of Kant's disciples including Hegel in German Idealism (A.2.e).
Schopenhauer's metaphysical system building on Kant addresses Kant's disciples in 19th-century philosophy (A.2.e).
Kant's critical philosophy directly addresses the foundational role of Immanuel Kant in German Idealism (A.2.e).
Darwin's theory connects to scientific developments and the principle of evolution (A.2.d) and scientific theory including new views of the universe (B.1.c).
Einstein's relativity directly addresses Einsteinian relativity in scientific theory (B.1.c).
Diplomatic history covers the Congress of Vienna (A.1.c.v), Realpolitik and diplomatic realignments (B.2), and WWI aftermath including Versailles (B.2.d.vi).
Capitalism from industrialization connects to the Industrial Revolution (A.2.a) and industrialization 1870-1914 (B.1.d).
Comparative economic development connects to the Industrial Revolution (A.2.a) and changing balance of economic power (B.1.d).
Modern warfare connects to World War I (B.2.d) including military technology and strategy.
Military strategy including Napoleon at Austerlitz connects to international war (A.1.c) and WWI strategic plans (B.2.d.v).
Air power development connects to new military technology in WWI including air power (B.2.d.i).
Welfare state development connects to Lloyd George's National Insurance Act (1911) (B.3.a.iv).
Aristocratic power and decline connects to the Third Estate revolt and abolition of the ancien regime (A.1.b.i).
Northern Ireland covers the Irish Rebellion of 1798 and union with Britain (A.1.e.ii) and the Irish Question, Fenianism, and Home Rule (B.3.a.iii).
Covers the Act of Union and Irish Rebellion context (A.1.e.ii), Great Famine and O'Connell (A.2.iii), Parnell and Home Rule (B.3.a.iii), and the Easter Rising (B.3.a.v).
Tocqueville connects to patriotism in America (A.1.a) and comparative analysis of American democracy and French political developments (A.2.j.ii).
Engels's historical materialism connects to German Idealism (A.2.e) and determinism and materialism (B.1.a).
Victorian Britain covers mid-Victorian politics and economics (B.3.a.i) and Victorian social attitudes (B.3.a.ii).
Co-evolution of weapons and tactics connects to new military technology in WWI (B.2.d.i).
British Empire connects to European imperialism resurgence (B.2.c) and Disraeli's imperialism (B.3.a.iii).
Scotland's Scottish Enlightenment and British Empire participation connect to mid-Victorian society (B.3.a.ii).
Decolonization theory connects to European imperialism resurgence (B.2.c) as the precondition for later independence.
Fascism's origins connect to the German Republic after WWI (B.3.c.v) and militant nationalism in Italy (B.3.f.ii).
Jewish nationalism from the 1870s connects to European imperialism resurgence (B.2.c).
The Holy Roman Empire's 1806 dissolution connects to Napoleon's reorganization of German states (A.1.c.ii) and the Confederation of the Rhine (A.1.i).

German literature from Sturm und Drang through Romanticism connects to the Romantic movement (A.2.c) and German Idealism (A.2.e), while the Franco-Prussian War era connects to German unification (B.3.c).
